title: Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu

infobox.value: BJJ, Gracie jiu-jitsu, gi jiu-jitsu, no-gi jiu-jitsu, [submission grappling](/wiki/Submission_grappling "Submission grappling") **Portuguese name:** *Jiu-jitsu brasileiro* **Japanese name:** *Burajirian jūjutsu* (ブラジリアン柔術)

Senjuro Kataoka (Kanaya), [Mataemon Tanabe](/wiki/Mataemon_Tanabe "Mataemon Tanabe"), [Taro Miyake](/wiki/Taro_Miyake "Taro Miyake"), [Yukio Tani](/wiki/Yukio_Tani "Yukio Tani"), [Sada Miyako](/wiki/Sada_Miyako "Sada Miyako"), [Geo Omori](/wiki/Geo_Omori "Geo Omori"), [Mitsuyo Maeda](/wiki/Mitsuyo_Maeda "Mitsuyo Maeda"), [Takeo Yano](/wiki/Takeo_Yano "Takeo Yano"), [Soshihiro Satake](/wiki/Soshihiro_Satake "Soshihiro Satake"), [Tokugoro Ito](/wiki/Tokugoro_Ito "Tokugoro Ito"), Jacyntho Ferro,[[1]](#cite_note-auto-1) Donato Pires dos Reis,[[1]](#cite_note-auto-1) [Hélio Gracie](/wiki/H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Hélio Gracie"), [Carlos Gracie](/wiki/Carlos_Gracie "Carlos Gracie"), George Gracie, Oswaldo Gracie, [Luiz França](/wiki/Luiz_Fran%C3%A7a "Luiz França"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Oswaldo Fadda](/wiki/Oswaldo_Fadda "Oswaldo Fadda")
[Submission wrestling](/wiki/Submission_wrestling "Submission wrestling"), [10th planet jiu-jitsu](/wiki/10th_planet_jiu-jitsu "10th planet jiu-jitsu")
*Jiu-jitsu brasileiro* **Japanese name:** *Burajirian jūjutsu* (ブラジリアン柔術) |

([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *jiu-jitsu brasileiro* [[ʒiw ˈʒitsu bɾaziˈlejɾu, ʒu -]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")), often abbreviated to **BJJ**, is a [self-defense](/wiki/Self-defense "Self-defense") system, [martial art](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts"), and [combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport") based on [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling"), [ground fighting](/wiki/Ground_fighting "Ground fighting"), and [submission holds](/wiki/Submission_holds "Submission holds"). It is primarily a ground-based fighting style and involves taking one's opponent down to the ground, gaining a dominant position, and then using a number of techniques to force them into [submission](/wiki/Submission_(combat_sports) "Submission (combat sports)") such as [joint locks](/wiki/Joint_lock "Joint lock"), [chokeholds](/wiki/Chokehold "Chokehold"), or [compression locks](/wiki/Compression_lock "Compression lock").

**Brazilian jiu-jitsu** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *jiu-jitsu brasileiro* [[ʒiw ˈʒitsu bɾaziˈlejɾu, ʒu -]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")), often abbreviated to **BJJ**, is a [self-defense](/wiki/Self-defense "Self-defense") system, [martial art](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts"), and [combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport") based on [grappling](/wiki/Grappling "Grappling"), [ground fighting](/wiki/Ground_fighting "Ground fighting"), and [submission holds](/wiki/Submission_holds "Submission holds"). It is primarily a ground-based fighting style and involves taking one's opponent down to the ground, gaining a dominant position, and then using a number of techniques to force them into [submission](/wiki/Submission_(combat_sports) "Submission (combat sports)") such as [joint locks](/wiki/Joint_lock "Joint lock"), [chokeholds](/wiki/Chokehold "Chokehold"), or [compression locks](/wiki/Compression_lock "Compression lock").

Brazilian jiu-jitsu was first developed by the Brazilian brothers [Carlos](/wiki/Carlos_Gracie "Carlos Gracie"), Oswaldo, Gastão Jr., and [Hélio Gracie](/wiki/H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Hélio Gracie") around 1925, after Carlos was taught [jujutsu](/wiki/Jujutsu "Jujutsu") and [catch wrestling](/wiki/Catch_wrestling "Catch wrestling") in 1917 by either [Mitsuyo Maeda](/wiki/Mitsuyo_Maeda "Mitsuyo Maeda"), a travelling Japanese judoka, or one of Maeda's students Jacyntho Ferro.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Later, the [Gracie family](/wiki/Gracie_family "Gracie family") developed their own self-defense system that they named Gracie jiu-jitsu. BJJ eventually became a distinct combat sport of its own through innovations and practices, and is considered essential for modern [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts").

Brazilian jiu-jitsu is based on the concept that a smaller, weaker person can successfully defend against a bigger, stronger opponent by using leverage and weight distribution. The focus is on taking the fight to the ground and using a number of holds and submissions to defeat them. [Sparring](/wiki/Sparring "Sparring"), commonly referred to as "rolling" within the BJJ community, and [live drilling](/wiki/Aliveness_(martial_arts) "Aliveness (martial arts)") play a significant role in training. In contrast to some other martial arts BJJ can be practiced using a [gi](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu_gi "Brazilian jiu-jitsu gi") or without it, also called 'no-gi', for this purpose [rash guards](/wiki/Rash_guard "Rash guard") are used. BJJ can also be used for [physical fitness](/wiki/Physical_fitness "Physical fitness"), building character or as a way of life.[[4]](#cite_note-stanlei13-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

The first public demonstration of jiu-jitsu in Brazil took place in 1906 in the city of [Manaus](/wiki/Manaus "Manaus"), the capital of [Amazonas](/wiki/Amazonas_(Brazilian_state) "Amazonas (Brazilian state)"). In November of that year, the ship *Jerome* docked in Manaus, carrying two Japanese martial artists who were touring the Americas to showcase their fighting style: jiu-jitsu master Akishima Sadashi and his assistant Suiotos Ki.

After settling in the city, the two men published a challenge in the local newspapers, inviting anyone willing to face them in public matches. An improvised arena was set up at the "Coliseu Metálico Brasileiro" circus in Praça da Saudade, where on November 18, Master Akishima and his disciple Suiotos fought over a dozen local challengers—defeating all of them in less than five minutes each, in front of a large crowd. This marked the first official jiu-jitsu matches on Brazilian soil.

After several months of exhibitions at the circus, Akishima and Suiotos left Amazonas and sailed to [Liverpool, England](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), aboard the ship *Antony*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1908, the Brazilian Navy ship [*Benjamin Constant*](/wiki/Brazilian_cruiser_Benjamin_Constant "Brazilian cruiser Benjamin Constant") rescued a group of castaways on a Pacific island, among them a Japanese jiu-jitsu instructor [Sada Miyako](/wiki/Sada_Miyako "Sada Miyako"). He and his disciple Kakiara were allowed to stay aboard and continue the journey to [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"). During the voyage, the two Japanese men taught jiu-jitsu techniques to the sailors.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Jiu-jitsu_contra_capoeira_%28detail%29.jpg/250px-Jiu-jitsu_contra_capoeira_%28detail%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jiu-jitsu_contra_capoeira_(detail).jpg)

[Panel](/wiki/Panel_(comics) "Panel (comics)") by Alfredo Storni featuring capoeirista [Ciríaco](/wiki/Francisco_da_Silva_Cir%C3%ADaco "Francisco da Silva Ciríaco") defeating [jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Jiu-jitsu "Jiu-jitsu") fighter [Sada Miyako](/wiki/Sada_Miyako "Sada Miyako") with a *[rabo de arraia](/wiki/Rabo_de_arraia "Rabo de arraia")* kick, [*O Malho*](/wiki/O_Malho "O Malho"), 1909.

Upon arriving in Rio de Janeiro aboard the *Benjamin Constant*, Miyako was hired by the Navy to train officers at Fortaleza de Villegagnon, later extending his teachings to Army personnel. He remained in Brazil for some time, giving private lessons, performing demonstrations, and engaging in public fights—most notably his 1909 match against capoeirista [Francisco da Silva Ciríaco](/wiki/Francisco_da_Silva_Cir%C3%ADaco "Francisco da Silva Ciríaco"), which became legendary.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Mitsuyo Maeda](/wiki/Mitsuyo_Maeda "Mitsuyo Maeda"), a distinguished judo black belt from the [Kodokan](/wiki/Kodokan "Kodokan"), trained under [Tomita Tsunejirō](/wiki/Tomita_Tsunejir%C5%8D "Tomita Tsunejirō")—the first student of judo's founder, [Kano Jigoro](/wiki/Kano_Jigoro "Kano Jigoro")—and embarked on a global mission to showcase and spread judo. Departing Japan in 1904, Maeda traveled to various countries, demonstrating his skills and taking on challengers like [wrestlers](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"), [boxers](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [savate](/wiki/Savate "Savate") fighters, and other martial artists. His journey led him to [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), where he arrived by November 14, 1914 (some sources record his arrival as early as September 1914).[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Maeda & Gracie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Maeda & Gracie")]

In Brazil, Maeda's demonstrations of "Kano jiu-jitsu"—a term then synonymous with judo—laid the groundwork for what would become Brazilian jiu-jitsu. In 1916, the American Circus in [Belém](/wiki/Bel%C3%A9m "Belém"), where Gastão Gracie was a business partner, hosted performances by the Queirolo Brothers, an [Italian-Argentine](/wiki/Italian-Argentine "Italian-Argentine") circus troupe, who introduced Maeda to the audience.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The following year, Gastão's eldest son, [Carlos Gracie](/wiki/Carlos_Gracie "Carlos Gracie"), attended one of Maeda's demonstrations at the Da Paz Theatre and was inspired to study the art.[[12]](#cite_note-GRACIE_FAMILY-12)

According to the [Gracie family](/wiki/Gracie_family "Gracie family")'s account, Carlos became a direct student of Maeda at his school, which was overseen by Jacyntho Ferro.[[1]](#cite_note-auto-1) However, some Brazilian jiu-jitsu practitioner historians like [Robert Drysdale](/wiki/Robert_Drysdale "Robert Drysdale") challenge this narrative, proposing that Carlos was primarily instructed by Ferro rather than Maeda himself.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Regardless, Carlos went on to share his knowledge with his brothers, including [Hélio Gracie](/wiki/H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Hélio Gracie"). Due to his smaller build, Hélio struggled with executing many judo throws and with judo techniques that relied on overpowering opponents. Instead, he refined the art, emphasizing [ground fighting](/wiki/Ground_fighting "Ground fighting") and leverage—key aspects of jiu-jitsu and judo's [ne-waza](/wiki/Ne-waza "Ne-waza")—over traditional throws. This adaptation became the cornerstone of Gracie jiu-jitsu.[[15]](#cite_note-Gracie_History_|_Gracie_Academy-15)

While the Gracie family is widely celebrated for popularizing Brazilian jiu-jitsu, another significant parallel lineage emerged through [Luiz França](/wiki/Luiz_Fran%C3%A7a "Luiz França") and gained prominence through his student [Oswaldo Fadda](/wiki/Oswaldo_Fadda "Oswaldo Fadda"). The França-Fadda lineage claims roots from [Soshihiro Satake](/wiki/Soshihiro_Satake "Soshihiro Satake"), [Geo Omori](/wiki/Geo_Omori "Geo Omori"), and [Mitsuyo Maeda](/wiki/Mitsuyo_Maeda "Mitsuyo Maeda"), although some practitioner historians like Drysdale speculate from data that França either learned from the Gracies or was self taught.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Equipe_Mestre_Wilson-18) Fadda's students were renowned for their mastery of [footlocks](/wiki/Leglock "Leglock") and for defeating the Gracies in notable gym battles.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Fadda's influence endures today through teams like [Nova União](/wiki/Nova_Uni%C3%A3o_(mixed_martial_arts) "Nova União (mixed martial arts)") and [Grappling Fight Team](/wiki/GFTeam "GFTeam").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Beyond the Gracie and França-Fadda lineages, Brazilian jiu-jitsu in Brazil has branched into several prominent schools, including Gracie Humaitá, [Gracie Barra](/wiki/Gracie_Barra "Gracie Barra"), [Carlson Gracie](/wiki/Carlson_Gracie "Carlson Gracie") Jiu-Jitsu, and [Alliance Jiu Jitsu](/wiki/Alliance_Jiu_Jitsu "Alliance Jiu Jitsu"). Each of these lineages traces its roots back to the teachings Mitsuyo Maeda introduced to Brazil over a century ago.

### Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Name")]

The name "jiu-jitsu" derives from an older [romanization](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") of its original spelling in the West; the modern [Hepburn romanization](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization") of [柔術](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9F%94%E8%A1%93 "wikt:柔術") is "jūjutsu".

When Maeda left Japan, [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") was still often referred to as "Kano jiu-jitsu",[[21]](#cite_note-name_b-21) or, even more generically, simply as *jiu-jitsu*.[[22]](#cite_note-name_c-22)[[23]](#cite_note-name_d-23) Higashi, the co-author of *[The Complete Kano Jiu-Jitsu (Judo)](/wiki/The_Complete_Kano_Jiu-Jitsu_(Judo) "The Complete Kano Jiu-Jitsu (Judo)")*[[21]](#cite_note-name_b-21) wrote in the foreword:

> Some confusion has arisen over the employment of the term 'jiudo'. To make the matter clear I will state that jiudo is the term selected by Professor Kano as describing his system more accurately than jiu-jitsu does. Professor Kano is one of the leading educators of Japan, and it is natural that he should cast about for the technical word that would most accurately describe his system. But the Japanese people generally still cling to the more popular nomenclature and call it jiu-jitsu.[[21]](#cite_note-name_b-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/H%C3%A9lio_Gracie_%281952%29.tif/lossy-page1-250px-H%C3%A9lio_Gracie_%281952%29.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:H%C3%A9lio_Gracie_(1952).tif)

Young [Hélio Gracie](/wiki/H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Hélio Gracie") in 1952.

Outside Japan, however, this distinction was noted even less. Thus, when Maeda and Satake arrived in Brazil in 1914, every newspaper announced their art as being "jiu-jitsu", despite both men being [Kodokan](/wiki/Kodokan "Kodokan") judoka.

It was not until 1925 that the Japanese government itself officially mandated that the correct name for the martial art taught in the Japanese public schools should be "judo" rather than "jujutsu".[[24]](#cite_note-24) In Brazil, the art is still called "jiu-jitsu". When the Gracies went to the United States and spread jiu-jitsu, they used the terms "Gracie jiu-jitsu" and non-Gracies using the term "Brazilian jiu-jitsu" to differentiate from the already present styles using similar-sounding names. In a 1994 interview with Yoshinori Nishi, Hélio Gracie said that he did not even know the word *judo* itself until the sport came in the 1950s to Brazil, because he heard that Mitsuyo Maeda called his style "jiu-jitsu".

The art is sometimes referred to as Gracie jiu-jitsu (GJJ), a name [trademarked](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark") by [Rorion Gracie](/wiki/Rorion_Gracie "Rorion Gracie"), but after a legal dispute with his cousin [Carley Gracie](/wiki/Carley_Gracie "Carley Gracie"), his trademark to the name was voided.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Other members of the Gracie family often call their style by personalized names, such as Ceaser Gracie Jiu-Jitsu or Renzo Gracie Jiu-Jitsu, and similarly, the [Machado family](/wiki/Machado_family "Machado family") call their style Machado Jiu-Jitsu (MJJ). While each style and its instructors have their own unique aspects, they are all basic variations of Brazilian jiu-jitsu.

Brazilian jiu-jitsu shares many techniques with the original [Kodokan judo](/wiki/Kodokan_judo "Kodokan judo"), especially certain styles practiced before judo became part of the Olympics such as [Kosen judo](/wiki/Kosen_judo "Kosen judo"),[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) and still practiced to a lesser extent,[[28]](#cite_note-28) as well as with some earlier *ryu* jujtsu schools with their ground work emphasys that were historical predecessors like Okayama newaza (Takenouchi/Fusen ryu), Oguri ryu, and Tenshin-Shinyo ryu. Because of this some practitioners have suggested that Brazilian jiu-jitsu should just be called "jiu-jitsu".[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Divergence from Kodokan Judo and Jujutsu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Divergence from Kodokan Judo and Jujutsu")]

Certain changes were made to the [rules of judo](/wiki/Judo_rules "Judo rules") after it was introduced to Brazil. Some of these rule changes sought to enhance it as a spectator sport, and to improve safety. Several of these rule changes de-emphasized the groundwork aspects of judo, and others have reduced the range of [joint locks](/wiki/Joint_lock "Joint lock") application. Brazilian jiu-jitsu since its inception did not strictly follow kodokan judo rules; this divergence helped BJJ develop a distinct identity becoming a groundwork and submission focus fighting style. This difference was later consolidated with the creation of a new set of rules that guide the BJJ practice today.[[30]](#cite_note-ibjjf.org-30)

Even though BJJ uses and allows many of the techniques found in judo and jujutsu, the evolution of these techniques in BJJ has followed a distinct path, giving primary emphasis to ground fighting positions and maneuvers, and having the [guard](/wiki/Guard_(grappling) "Guard (grappling)") as the central working point of the fight. This is reflected on how different fighting techniques are scored in competition. In BJJ positions such as the rear mount receive a higher score, and the focus of the fight is to establish a dominant position against the adversary on the ground and submit them using chokeholds or joint locks. By contrast in judo, throwing techniques are scored higher and can give a direct victory if executed correctly, the goal of the fight is to sweep or throw the opponent to the ground to win.

BJJ also allows heelhooks and knee-reaping which are prohibited in judo, and any takedowns used in wrestling, sambo, or other grappling arts, including direct attempts to take down by touching the legs or dragging the opponent to the ground.[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Spinal locks](/wiki/Spinal_lock "Spinal lock") and [cervical locks](/wiki/Cervical_lock "Cervical lock") are not allowed in gi jiu-jitsu,[[30]](#cite_note-ibjjf.org-30) amateur MMA,[[32]](#cite_note-abcboxing.com-32) multiple forms of no-gi jiu-jitsu,[[30]](#cite_note-ibjjf.org-30) Judo,[[33]](#cite_note-intjudo.eu-33) and other martial arts,[[34]](#cite_note-usgrappling.com-34) due to their potential to cause serious injury. BJJ also has become more "sports-oriented" in the 21st century, prohibiting techniques such as slams.[[35]](#cite_note-USGR-35) Another divergence of BJJ from judo and jujutsu is that BJJ allows no-gi practice and competition, with its own subset of rules. Use of holds and takedowns from wrestling and other grappling styles is common in no-gi BJJ, and light strikes may also be used when the competition rules permit, such as the open palm strike in CJJ ([Combat jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Combat_jiu-jitsu "Combat jiu-jitsu")).[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Prominence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Prominence")]

In 1972, [Carlos Gracie](/wiki/Carlos_Gracie "Carlos Gracie") moved to the United States to teach Brazilian jiu-jitsu, and in 1978 was followed by [Rorion Gracie](/wiki/Rorion_Gracie "Rorion Gracie"), who co-founded the [Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship") (UFC) in 1993.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Jiu-jitsu came to international prominence in martial arts circles when Brazilian jiu-jitsu expert [Royce Gracie](/wiki/Royce_Gracie "Royce Gracie") won the [first](/wiki/UFC_1 "UFC 1"), [second](/wiki/UFC_2 "UFC 2") and [fourth](/wiki/UFC_4 "UFC 4") Ultimate Fighting Championships, which at the time were [single elimination](/wiki/Single-elimination_tournament "Single-elimination tournament") martial arts tournaments. Royce fought successfully against several larger opponents proficient in other fighting styles, including [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [shootfighting](/wiki/Shoot-fighting "Shoot-fighting"), [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_thai "Muay thai"), [karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate"), [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"), and [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo"). BJJ has since become an elementary aspect of [MMA](/wiki/MMA "MMA"), revealing the importance of [ground fighting](/wiki/Ground_fighting "Ground fighting") in a fight. Sport BJJ tournaments continue to grow in popularity and have given rise to no-gi [submission grappling](/wiki/Submission_grappling "Submission grappling") tournaments, such as the [ADCC Submission Wrestling World Championship](/wiki/ADCC_Submission_Wrestling_World_Championship "ADCC Submission Wrestling World Championship") and [NAGA](/w/index.php?title=North_American_Grappling_Association&action=edit&redlink=1 "North American Grappling Association (page does not exist)"), the North American Grappling Association. Sport BJJ has also become a popular method of fitness around the world in recent years.[[39]](#cite_note-39) By the early 2000s, Brazilian jiu-jitsu became a sport that is present worldwide.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Style of fighting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Style of fighting")]

See also: [List of Brazilian jiu-jitsu techniques](/wiki/List_of_Brazilian_jiu-jitsu_techniques "List of Brazilian jiu-jitsu techniques")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Brazilian jiu-jitsu focuses on getting an opponent to the ground in order to neutralize possible strength or size advantages through [ground fighting](/wiki/Ground_fighting "Ground fighting") techniques and [submission holds](/wiki/Submission_holds "Submission holds") involving [joint-locks](/wiki/Joint-locks "Joint-locks") and [chokeholds](/wiki/Chokehold "Chokehold"). On the ground, physical strength can be offset or enhanced through proper grappling techniques.

BJJ (Brazilian jiu-jitsu) employs a wide range of takedown techniques to bring an opponent to the ground such as "[pulling guard](/wiki/Guard_(grappling)#Pulling_Guard "Guard (grappling)")", which is not used in other combat sports such as judo or wrestling. Once the opponent is on the ground, a number of manoeuvres (and counter-manoeuvres) are available to manipulate the opponent into a suitable position for the application of a submission technique. Achieving a dominant position on the ground is one of the hallmarks of BJJ, which includes effective use of the [guard](/wiki/Guard_(grappling) "Guard (grappling)") position to defend oneself from bottom (using both submissions and sweeps, with sweeps leading to the possibility of dominant position or an opportunity to pass the guard), and passing the guard to dominate from top position with [side control](/wiki/Side_control "Side control"), [mount](/wiki/Mount_(grappling) "Mount (grappling)"), and [back mount](/wiki/Back_mount "Back mount") positions. This system of manoeuvring and manipulation can be likened to a form of kinetic or physical chess when executed by two experienced practitioners.[[41]](#cite_note-41) A submission hold in BJJ is often likened to the equivalent of "checkmate", where the opponent is left with no other option but to tap, be injured, or choked.

[Renzo Gracie](/wiki/Renzo_Gracie "Renzo Gracie") wrote in his book *Mastering JUJITSU*:

> "The classical jujutsu of old Japan appeared to have no common strategy to guide a combatant over the course of a fight. Indeed, this was one of Kano's most fundamental and perceptive criticisms of the classical program." Maeda not only taught the art of judo to [Carlos Gracie](/wiki/Carlos_Gracie "Carlos Gracie"), but also taught a particular philosophy about the nature of combat developed by Kano, and further refined by Maeda based on his worldwide travels competing against fighters skilled in a wide variety of martial arts.[[42]](#cite_note-renzog1-42)

The book details Maeda's theory as arguing that physical combat could be broken down into distinct phases, such as the striking phase, the grappling phase, the ground phase, etc. Thus, it was a smart fighter's task to keep the fight located in the phase of combat best suited to his own strengths. Renzo Gracie stated that this was a fundamental influence of the Gracie approach to combat. These strategies were further developed over time by the Gracie family, among others, and became prominent in contemporary MMA.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Training methods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Training methods")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu_strengthens_camaraderie%2C_build_trust_at_Camp_Lemonnier_140727-F-SJ695-033.jpg/250px-Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu_strengthens_camaraderie%2C_build_trust_at_Camp_Lemonnier_140727-F-SJ695-033.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu_strengthens_camaraderie,_build_trust_at_Camp_Lemonnier_140727-F-SJ695-033.jpg)

A Brazilian jiu-jitsu instructor demonstrates how to tackle an opponent.

As BJJ focuses on submissions, sparring and live drilling ("rolling") become the most essential part of the training regime. This type of training allows practitioners to practice at full speed and with full strength, resembling the effort made in a competition. Training methods include drills in which techniques are practiced against a non-resisting and resisting partners; isolation sparring (commonly referred to as positional drilling) where only a certain technique or sets of techniques are used; and full sparring where each practitioner tries to submit their opponent through technique. Physical conditioning is also an important aspect of training.

The [Gracie family](/wiki/Gracie_family "Gracie family") lineage descended from Helio Gracie focuses on practical applications of BJJ that apply primarily to self-defense.[[44]](#cite_note-44) They will often run development drills in which a person is surrounded by a circle of other students who will attempt to attack the defending student, who in turn must defend themselves using techniques.

### Etiquette & Customs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Etiquette & Customs")]

Brazilian jiu-jitsu gyms typically follow a set of ground rules to instill trust, respect, and cleanliness amongst students. While some gyms adhere to less formalities than others, some still follow traditional customs. Common practices include:

* Taking shoes off before stepping on the mat.
* Bowing before and after stepping on the mat.
* Lining up in rank order before and after class.
* Teaching or explaining techniques to lower ranked students.
* Addressing the teacher as coach or professor.
* Keeping your gi closed with a belt.
* Bowing or shaking hands before and after sparring.
* Covering any cuts with tape or gauze.
* Having good hygiene practices including brushing teeth, having a clean gi, and wearing deodorant.
* Respecting rolling with upper belts as a privilege.

## Primary ground positions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Primary ground positions")]

Main article: [Grappling position](/wiki/Grappling_position "Grappling position")

Once on the ground, the BJJ practitioner strives to take a dominant or controlling position from where to apply submissions. These positions provide different submission or transition options.

### Side control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Side control")]

Main article: [Side control](/wiki/Side_control "Side control")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Udegarami.JPG/250px-Udegarami.JPG)](/wiki/File:Udegarami.JPG)

An Americana armlock submission from traditional [side control](/wiki/Side_control "Side control").

In side control (also known as *side mount*, *cross-side* and *cem quilos* — "a hundred kilograms" in Brazilian Portuguese),[[45]](#cite_note-45) the practitioner pins their opponent to the ground from the side of their torso. The top grappler lies across the opponent, with weight applied to the opponent's chest. The opponent may be further controlled by pressure on either side of the shoulders and hips from the practitioner's elbows, shoulders, and knees. A wide variety of submissions can be initiated from side control. Additionally, the typical side control increases the opportunity for the top-side grappler to advance to other dominant positions. This position is often used in MMA as it allows the top fighter to strike whilst overcoming their opponents' defense. There are many variants of the side control position, including [kesa gatame](/wiki/Kesa_gatame "Kesa gatame"), *reverse side control* (or *switch-base*), and others.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Knee-on-belly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Knee-on-belly")]

Main article: [Knee-on-stomach](/wiki/Knee-on-stomach "Knee-on-stomach")

The knee-on-belly position (also known as *knee ride*) is a modified side pin that is distinctly separated from the side control position. The knee-on-belly position is characterized primarily by the control of the opponent with one leg out for base and balance and the other leg positioned across the opponent's torso, pinning them to the ground. This form of pin is a mobile pin rather than a static pin, and is considered a more dominant position in many grappling formats. The knee-on-belly position is worth additional points because it provides a greater striking platform than traditional side control and is closer to achieving the more ideal mounted position.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Full mount

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Full mount")]

Main article: [Mount (grappling)](/wiki/Mount_(grappling) "Mount (grappling)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Andrea_Galvao.jpg/250px-Andrea_Galvao.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrea_Galvao.jpg)

[Full mount](/wiki/Mount_(grappling) "Mount (grappling)") is considered one of the most dominant [grappling positions](/wiki/Grappling_position "Grappling position").

In the mount (or full mount) position, the practitioner sits astride the opponent's front torso or chest, controlling the opponent with body-weight and hips. In this position, the person attacking from mount can isolate their opponent's arms (bringing them overhead), which creates a strong attacking position. Full Mount can be used to apply a variety of submissions, including armlocks or chokes. There are different types of mount — depending on body positioning — including *low-mount*, *mid-mount*, *high-mount*, *S-mount*, and *technical mount*.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Back mount

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Back mount")]

Main article: [Back mount](/wiki/Back_mount "Back mount")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Back_mount.jpg/250px-Back_mount.jpg)](/wiki/File:Back_mount.jpg)

Back mount, also considered one of the most dominant positions in BJJ

When taking the back mount position (often known in Brazilian jiu-jitsu as *back control* or *attacking the back*), the practitioner attaches to the back of the opponent by wrapping his legs around and hooking the opponent's thighs with their heel,[[30]](#cite_note-ibjjf.org-30) or locking in a body triangle by crossing one shin across the waist like a belt then placing the back of the opposing knee over the instep as if finishing a triangle choke. Simultaneously, the upper body is controlled by wrapping the arms around the chest or neck of the opponent. This position is often used to apply [chokeholds](/wiki/Chokeholds "Chokeholds"), as well as armlocks and triangles, and neutralizes an opponent's potential size or strength advantage.

### North south

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: North south")]

The North South position occurs when a practitioner is lying on top of their opponent, with their weight over their opponent's chest and head area, with their legs pointing away from their opponent. Control is established by controlling the opponent's head and/or arms.[[49]](#cite_note-49) As with most top controlling positions in BJJ, the top practitioner applies pressure by bringing their hips downwards towards the ground, generating what is referred to as *dead weight*. There are several submissions and transitions that are possible from the North South position, most commonly the North South choke, North South [kimura](/wiki/Kimura_lock "Kimura lock"), and others.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Guards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Guards")]

Main article: [Guard (grappling)](/wiki/Guard_(grappling) "Guard (grappling)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Brazilian_Jiu-jitsu-Closed_guard.jpg/250px-Brazilian_Jiu-jitsu-Closed_guard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brazilian_Jiu-jitsu-Closed_guard.jpg)

The Jiu-Jitsu practitioner in blue is demonstrating a type of closed [guard](/wiki/Guard_(grappling) "Guard (grappling)")

When in the "Guard" position, the practitioner is on their back controlling an opponent with his legs. The bottom practitioner pushes and pulls with the legs or feet to unbalance and limit the movements of his opponent. This position allows practitioners a wide variety of counter-attacks from the bottom position, including submissions and sweeps.

The three most common types of guards include the Closed Guard, [Half Guard](/wiki/Half_guard "Half guard"), and Open Guard.

In closed guard, the bottom grappler has his legs around the opponent's hips, with ankles closed together to control their opponent. The closed guard can be an effective position. This guard allows many setups for submissions such as joint locks and chokes, as well as sweeps. In the open guard, the legs are not hooked together and the bottom grappler uses his legs or feet to push or pull their opponent.

There are many variations of open guard with distinct names and positioning including the Butterfly Guard, De La Riva Guard, X-Guard, K-Guard, [Rubber guard](/wiki/Rubber_guard "Rubber guard"), Spider Guard, Octopus Guard, Lapel Guard, Worm Guard (made popular by [Keenan Cornelius](/wiki/Keenan_Cornelius "Keenan Cornelius")) and others. Butterfly guard is when the bottom grappler brings his legs up and feet together against the inner thighs of top opponent. The name is derived from the resulting butterfly wing shape. Butterfly guard increases both space to maneuver and the ability to counter the opponent with the shins or arches of the feet against the competitor's inner thighs. Spider guard is when the bottom grappler puts one or two of his feet on his opponents arms, this guard is effective at keeping control on the bottom and can be used to set up sweeps or submissions.

In the half guard, one of the top grappler's legs is controlled by the bottom grappler's legs, preventing the top opponent from passing side control or full mount positions. There is also a variant of half guard called "50/50 guard", which consist of each opponent usually in sitting positions with one of their legs hooking the same leg of their opponent in a mirrored fashion. This position is called 50/50 because neither opponent has a distinct advantage, where both sides have the same possibilities of sweeps and attacks.

Another variation of the half guard position is the "deep half guard", which involves the bottom grappler positioning themselves underneath their opponent, grabbing the top grappler's thigh. This gives the individual on the bottom the opportunity to sweep their opponent, and end up on a more dominant, top position. This position was popularized by American BJJ Black Belt, [Jeff Glover](/wiki/Jeff_Glover "Jeff Glover").[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The De La Riva Guard (DLR) is an open guard technique in Brazilian jiu-jitsu, named after the competitor Ricardo De La Riva. While not an entirely new concept in grappling, as it had origins in nonatei style judo credited to Oda Tsunetane, it gained prominence through De La Riva's use. In Brazilian jiu-jitsu, the DLR Guard is identified when a competitor lies on their back, controlling their opponent with open legs. This position is defined by one leg hooking outside the opponent's leg (e.g., the guard player's left leg hooking the passer's right leg), thereby compromising the passer's balance.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

## Submissions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Submissions")]

For a list of list of Brazilian jiu-jitsu techniques, see [List of Brazilian jiu-jitsu techniques](/wiki/List_of_Brazilian_jiu-jitsu_techniques "List of Brazilian jiu-jitsu techniques").

The majority of submission holds can be grouped into two broad categories: [joint locks](/wiki/Joint_lock "Joint lock") and [chokes](/wiki/Chokehold "Chokehold"). Joint locks typically involve isolating an opponent's limb and creating a lever with the body position, which will force the joint to move past its normal range of motion.[[53]](#cite_note-mma-53) Pressure is increased in a controlled manner and released if the opponent cannot escape the hold and signals defeat by [tapping](/wiki/Submission_(combat_sport) "Submission (combat sport)"). A choke hold can disrupt the blood supply to the brain and cause unconsciousness if the opponent does not tap when required.

### Compression locks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Compression locks")]

*See also* [Compression lock](/wiki/Compression_lock "Compression lock")

A less common type of submission hold is a [compression lock](/wiki/Compression_lock "Compression lock"), where the muscle of an opponent is compressed against a hard, large bone (commonly the shin or forearm), causing significant pain to the opponent. These types of locks are not usually allowed in competition due to the high risk of tearing muscle tissue. This type of lock also often hyper-extends the joint in the opposite direction, pulling it apart. Some compression locks include the [Achilles lock](/wiki/Compression_lock#Achilles_lock "Compression lock"), [Biceps slicer](/wiki/Compression_lock#Biceps_slicer "Compression lock"), and [Leg slicer](/wiki/Compression_lock#Leg_slicer "Compression lock") (or Calf slicer).

### Joint locks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Joint locks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/In-guard_armbar.jpg/250px-In-guard_armbar.jpg)](/wiki/File:In-guard_armbar.jpg)

A practitioner attempting an [armbar](/wiki/Armbar "Armbar") submission

While many joint locks are permitted in BJJ, most competitions ban or restrict some or all joint locks that involve the knees, ankles, and spine. The reason for this is that the angles of manipulation required to cause pain are nearly the same as those that would cause serious injury. Joint locks that require a twisting motion of the knee (such as heel hooks) are usually banned in gi competitions because successfully completing the move can frequently result in permanent damage, often requiring surgery. Similarly, [joint manipulations of the spine](/wiki/Neck_Crank "Neck Crank") are typically barred due to the inherent danger of crushing or misaligning [cervical vertebrae](/wiki/Cervical_vertebrae "Cervical vertebrae"). Leglocks are allowed in varying degrees depending on skill level, with the most prominent BJJ tournaments typically allowing only the straight ankle lock and muscle stretching submissions such as the "banana split" from white through purple belt, with the kneebar, toehold, and calf slicer submissions being permitted at brown and black belt. [Heel hooks](/wiki/Heel_hooks "Heel hooks") and knee-reaping are illegal in gi BJJ, since they are considered to be exceptionally dangerous techniques, but are allowed by [IBJJF](/wiki/International_Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu_Federation "International Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu Federation") rules in no-gi competitions.[[35]](#cite_note-USGR-35)[[54]](#cite_note-54) Most joint locks involving the wrist, elbow, shoulder or ankle are permitted as there is a great deal more flexibility in those joints and those locks are usually safe.

Joint locks include armbars, kimuras, Americanas, straight-arm lock, omoplata, marceloplata, tarikoplata, banana split (or electric chair), twister, wrist lock, heel hook, toehold, kneebar, straight ankle lock, and others.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Chokes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Chokes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/MCMAP_Rear_choke_-_Defense_Visual_Information_Center_2006.jpg/250px-MCMAP_Rear_choke_-_Defense_Visual_Information_Center_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:MCMAP_Rear_choke_-_Defense_Visual_Information_Center_2006.jpg)

A [rear naked choke](/wiki/Rear_naked_choke "Rear naked choke") or "*mata-leão"* in Brazilian Portuguese (*lion killer choke*) as demonstrated by a [MCMAP](/wiki/MCMAP "MCMAP") instructor

[Chokeholds](/wiki/Chokehold "Chokehold") or *chokes* are a common form of submission. They can be applied either by using the gi as the method of attack (e.g. the [bow-and-arrow choke](/wiki/Okuri_eri_jime "Okuri eri jime")), or by using a combination of head and arm, or even leg pressure (e.g. the [triangle choke](/wiki/Triangle_choke "Triangle choke")).[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In BJJ, strangles are used to put pressure on the [carotid](/wiki/Carotid "Carotid") arteries, and may also apply pressure to the nerve [baroreceptors](/wiki/Baroreceptor "Baroreceptor") in the neck. This kind of strangle is very fast acting (if done properly) with victims typically losing consciousness in a matter of seconds.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

In contrast, some techniques function as an air choke (involving constriction of the [windpipe](/wiki/Windpipe "Windpipe")), such as an *Ezekiel choke* or [Sode guruma jime](/wiki/Sode_guruma_jime "Sode guruma jime"). Many techniques can, in effect, be a choke or a strangle (or both) depending on how they are applied.

Strangles include [rear-naked choke](/wiki/Rear-naked_choke "Rear-naked choke"), [bow-and-arrow choke](/wiki/Okuri_eri_jime "Okuri eri jime"), [triangle](/wiki/Sankaku-jime "Sankaku-jime"), Guillotine choke, [sleeve choke](/wiki/Sode_guruma_jime "Sode guruma jime") (or Ezekiel choke), cross collar choke (or X choke), [baseball choke](/wiki/Kata_juji_jime#variants "Kata juji jime"), Clock choke, D'Arce choke, Anaconda choke, [arm triangle choke](/wiki/Arm_triangle_choke "Arm triangle choke"), north–south choke, crucifix choke, [gogoplata](/wiki/Gogoplata "Gogoplata"), loop choke, [lapel half nelson choke](/wiki/Kata_ha_jime "Kata ha jime"), and others.

## Uniform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Uniform")]

Main article: [Brazilian jiu-jitsu gi](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu_gi "Brazilian jiu-jitsu gi")

The Brazilian jiu-jitsu practitioner's uniform is commonly referred to as *[gi](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu_gi "Brazilian jiu-jitsu gi")* or *[kimono](/wiki/Kimono "Kimono")* is similar to a [judogi](/wiki/Judogi "Judogi"), but with slight differences in the dimensions and often made of lighter material with tighter cuffs on the pants and jacket. This allows the practitioner to benefit from a closer fit, providing less material for an opponent to manipulate. Traditionally, to be [promoted](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu_ranking_system "Brazilian jiu-jitsu ranking system") in Brazilian jiu-jitsu, the wearing of the jiu-jitsu gi while training is a requirement. Recently with the growing popularity of "no-gi" Brazilian jiu-jitsu has the practice of giving out belts to no-gi practitioners (e.g., [Rolles Gracie](/wiki/Rolles_Gracie "Rolles Gracie") awarding [Rashad Evans](/wiki/Rashad_Evans "Rashad Evans") a black belt) has become more common.

There are certain differences between [gi](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu_gi "Brazilian jiu-jitsu gi") jiu-jitsu and "no-gi" jiu-jitsu. In gi jiu-jitsu one can grip an opponent's uniform, using it to submit or advance position. There are a number of submissions that are specific to the gi, such as the "Loop choke", "Collar choke", and others. A specific set of rules to guide no-gi competitions is issued by the [IBJJF](/wiki/International_Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu_Federation "International Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu Federation"), but there may be variation in the set of rules applied in each competition. By IBJJF rules uniform grips are not permitted in "no-gi" jiu-jitsu.[[30]](#cite_note-ibjjf.org-30) BJJ athletes who practice no-gi usually wear a rash guard and MMA shorts with spats underneath.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

The Brazilian jiu-jitsu ranking system awards a practitioner different coloured belts to signify increasing levels of technical knowledge and practical skill. While the system's structure shares its origins with the [judo ranking system](/wiki/Judo#Rank_and_grading "Judo") and the origins of all coloured belts, it now contains many of its own unique aspects and themes. Some of these differences are relatively minor, such as the division between youth and adult belts and the stripe/degree system. Others are quite distinct and have become synonymous with the art, such as a higher degree of informality in promotional criteria, a focus on competitive demonstration of skill, and a conservative approach to promotion in general.[[59]](#cite_note-Gracie1-59)

Traditionally, the concept of competitive skill demonstration as a quickened and earned route of promotion holds true.[[60]](#cite_note-IBJJF_Graduation_System-60)[[59]](#cite_note-Gracie1-59) Some schools have placed a green belt for adults between the white and blue belt ranks due to the long periods between advancement. In addition, the use of a grey belt has been instituted for many children's programs to signal progress between the white and yellow belt rankings.

A [black belt](/wiki/Black_belt_(martial_arts) "Black belt (martial arts)") in Brazilian jiu-jitsu commonly takes more than several years to earn, and the rank is generally considered expert level. The amount of time it takes to achieve the rank of black belt varies between the practitioner. Some notable individuals who had previous backgrounds in other martial arts have been promoted directly to black belt rank without going through any intermediate rank, though this has fallen out of favour in recent times.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Others have achieved the rank in relatively short time frames.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Outside of exceptions such as these, the average time frame is around 10 years with a consistent training schedule.[[63]](#cite_note-63) However, Ryron Gracie (grandson/grandnephew of founders [Hélio](/wiki/H%C3%A9lio_Gracie "Hélio Gracie") and [Carlos Gracie](/wiki/Carlos_Gracie "Carlos Gracie")) has stated that the average of 10–12 years is longer than necessary, suggesting that the [ego](/wiki/Ego_(Freudian) "Ego (Freudian)") of the practitioner often hinders progress, and advancement to black belt should take about 7 years.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

## Federations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Federations")]

Since its inception Brazilian jiu-jitsu has had different registered federations and tournaments. The first jiu-jitsu federation was the [Jiu-Jitsu Federation of Guanabara](/wiki/Jiu-Jitsu_Federation_of_Guanabara "Jiu-Jitsu Federation of Guanabara"), which has remained a regional federation of Rio de Janeiro, while many others were founded. Among the most prestigious of the many federations are the [Sport Jiu Jitsu International Federation (SJJIF)](/wiki/Sport_Jiu_Jitsu_International_Federation_(SJJIF) "Sport Jiu Jitsu International Federation (SJJIF)"), a nonprofit organization with federations and tournaments around the globe with the mission of making jiu-jitsu an [Olympic sport](/wiki/Olympic_sport "Olympic sport"), the [International Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu Federation](/wiki/International_Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu_Federation "International Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu Federation"), a for-profit organization that hosts a number of tournaments[[65]](#cite_note-ibjjf-65) and JJGF, founded by [Rickson Gracie](/wiki/Rickson_Gracie "Rickson Gracie"), which has focus on both BJJ as self-defense and as a sport.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

## Tournaments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Tournaments")]

While there are numerous local and regional tournaments administered regularly by private individuals and academies, there are two major entities in jiu-jitsu circles. First, is the [Sport Jiu Jitsu International Federation (SJJIF)](/wiki/Sport_Jiu_Jitsu_International_Federation_(SJJIF) "Sport Jiu Jitsu International Federation (SJJIF)"), a (nonprofit) organization with international federations and tournaments. Second, is the [International Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu Federation](/wiki/International_Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu_Federation "International Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu Federation") (IBJJF), a for-profit company that hosts a number of major tournaments worldwide. These include the [Pan American Championship](/wiki/Pan-American_Championship_(jiu-jitsu) "Pan-American Championship (jiu-jitsu)"), [European Championship](/wiki/European_Championship_(Brazilian_jiu-jitsu) "European Championship (Brazilian jiu-jitsu)"), and the [Mundials](/wiki/Mundials "Mundials"). [California](/wiki/California "California"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), and [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") are the three states in the US which host tournaments most frequently. Other promotions within [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), such as Battleground Grappling Championship, American Grappling Federation (AGF), North American BJJ Federation (NABJJF), and [North American Grappling Association](/w/index.php?title=North_American_Grappling_Association&action=edit&redlink=1 "North American Grappling Association (page does not exist)") (NAGA) host tournaments nationwide, but visit these states multiple times within a tournament season.

Another tournament to spring from the founding Gracie lineage is the Gracie Nationals or Gracie Worlds. Founded in 2007 by Rose Gracie, daughter of [Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship") creator and Brazilian jiu-jitsu grandmaster [Rorion Gracie](/wiki/Rorion_Gracie "Rorion Gracie"). Gracie Nationals/Worlds followed the guidelines of other major tournaments of the time, implementing a points systems

In 2012, the Gracie Worlds introduced a new submission-only format, removing certain judging systems that many interpreted as an outdated scoring system. Rose spoke about this change when she said, "Today's tournaments aren't what my grandfather [Helio Gracie] envisioned. There's so many rules that it takes away from the actual art of jiu-jitsu. We don't see many submissions. We see cheating, we see decisions made by a referee. We need to stand together against this and support a submission only kind of revolution." Cheating in jiu-jitsu, Rose said, comes in many forms. "[A competitor] will earn a point, then hold for the entire match so they can win with that one little advantage they got at the start," Rose said. "That's not jiu-jitsu. That's cheating." Mark McDonnell from Gracie Botany agrees.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

This discontent with points-based and advantage-style competition has been echoed throughout the jiu-jitsu community, leading to many prominent submission-only style events. At these events, the winner of a match is determined only by submission, and these tournaments at times have no time limit, or are timed with a result of double disqualification if there is no submission victory. This form of tournament have yet to become widespread, but is gaining in popularity especially amongst MMA competitors. [Metamoris](/wiki/Metamoris "Metamoris"), a grappling competition event run by Rose's brother [Ralek Gracie](/wiki/Ralek_Gracie "Ralek Gracie"), has helped advocate this tournament form. Another notable example of a submission-only format is the EBI Eddie Bravo Invitational, which was the first televised event of its kind, and is now being featured on [UFC Fight Pass](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Pass "UFC Fight Pass"). Other submission-only events have cropped up all over the world including TUFF invitational, [Polaris Pro Grappling](/wiki/Polaris_Pro_Grappling "Polaris Pro Grappling") based in the United Kingdom, [Submission Underground](/w/index.php?title=Submission_Underground&action=edit&redlink=1 "Submission Underground (page does not exist)") backed by longtime MMA star [Chael Sonnen](/wiki/Chael_Sonnen "Chael Sonnen"), and the [QUINTET](/wiki/Quintet_(grappling) "Quintet (grappling)") promotion, devised by Japanese MMA legend [Kazushi Sakuraba](/wiki/Kazushi_Sakuraba "Kazushi Sakuraba") and based around teams of five players each representing a different gym or background.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

At tournaments, especially those hosted by the [International Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu Federation](/wiki/International_Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu_Federation "International Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu Federation") (IBJJF), some athletes engage in a practice called "closing out".[[70]](#cite_note-graciemag.com-70) This occurs when two athletes, usually friends or members of the same team, meet in a match but refuse to fight. Tournament organizers can minimize the chance of close-outs by placing athletes from the same team on opposite sides of the bracket, a practice which makes the finals the only match in which they could possibly meet (assuming each team has no more than two competitors in a given bracket). When two athletes close out a match, they agree which one of them will technically forfeit to the other. As this most often occurs in the final matches of tournaments, this usually means deciding which of the two will win the gold medal and which will win the silver. Notable examples of closing out include Marcus "Buchecha" Almeida forfeiting the finals of the Absolute division at the 2018 IBJJF World Championship to his friend Leandro Lo, who had suffered a shoulder injury during a previous match,[[71]](#cite_note-71) and frequent close outs between the brothers Paulo and Joao Miyao, who often compete in the same weight division.[[72]](#cite_note-72)
Proponents of close-outs claim it fosters team unity and allows competitors to train as hard and as freely as they can with their teammates. They say that facing a teammate in a competition would make them hold back in training. Critics of the practice say it diminishes audience enjoyment of tournaments where it is allowed,[[70]](#cite_note-graciemag.com-70) and some tournaments, notably the ADCC, ban it.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

## Health considerations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Health considerations")]

### Safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Safety")]

Brazilian jiu-jitsu is one of the safest full contact martial arts. Its injury rate is around 9~39 per 1000 athlete exposures.[[74]](#cite_note-:0-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) This is lower than in [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts") (236~286 per 1000), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") (210~420 per 1000), [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") (25~131 per 1000) and [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo") (21~140 per 1000). It is similar to [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"), which also uses grappling instead of striking. The few injuries that may be incurred in Brazilian jiu-jitsu usually affect the joints and rarely the head. The most common injuries Brazilian jiu-jitsu practitioners suffer from are [ACL tears](/wiki/ACL_tear "ACL tear"), [rotator cuff](/wiki/Rotator_cuff "Rotator cuff") tears in the shoulders, and [spinal disc herniations](/wiki/Spinal_disc_hernia "Spinal disc hernia"), most commonly in the neck region. Many are repairable via surgery that require an extended rehab period before the athlete can return to BJJ training. Also many athletes suffer from minor injuries, such as elbow, finger, and wrist [tendonitis](/wiki/Tendonitis "Tendonitis"), due to overtraining and the grappling nature of the martial art, which can be strenuous and taxing for the joints and the tendons.

### Skin health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Skin health")]

Besides the normal strains and pulls associated with most martial arts, Brazilian jiu-jitsu practitioners (along with wrestlers, judoka, and other grapplers) are exposed to regular skin abrasions and potential unsanitary mat conditions. They are thus at higher risk for developing skin disease.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Several commonly contracted skin diseases include [ringworm](/wiki/Dermatophytosis "Dermatophytosis"), [impetigo](/wiki/Impetigo "Impetigo"), [herpes gladiatorum](/wiki/Herpes_gladiatorum "Herpes gladiatorum"), and [staph infection](/wiki/Staphylococcal_infection "Staphylococcal infection").[[77]](#cite_note-77) Proper hygiene practices, including regular cleaning of classroom mats, showering immediately after class with [soap](/wiki/Soap "Soap"), disinfecting and covering any open wounds, thorough cleaning of any [gi](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu_gi "Brazilian jiu-jitsu gi")/[rashguard](/wiki/Rashguard "Rashguard")/[headgear](/wiki/Wrestling_headgear "Wrestling headgear") used before the next class, not sharing used towels/uniforms, and using a [barrier cream](/wiki/Barrier_cream "Barrier cream") greatly reduces the chance of contracting a disease.

### Cauliflower ear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Cauliflower ear")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Dom_Cruz_2.png/250px-Dom_Cruz_2.png)](/wiki/File:Dom_Cruz_2.png)

Dominick Cruz's cauliflower ears

Due to the use of the head to maintain position and attack in jiu-jitsu, the ears can easily be damaged and begin to swell. Without immediate medical treatment, the cartilage in a swollen ear will separate from the [perichondrium](/wiki/Perichondrium "Perichondrium") that supplies its nutrients and will become permanently swollen/deformed ([cauliflower ear](/wiki/Cauliflower_ear "Cauliflower ear")). [Wrestling headgear](/wiki/Wrestling_headgear "Wrestling headgear") is sometimes used for the prevention of this condition. Treatment includes draining the hematoma or surgery.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

### Performance-enhancing drugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Performance-enhancing drugs")]

The practice of taking [performance-enhancing drugs](/wiki/Performance-enhancing_drugs "Performance-enhancing drugs"), including [anabolic steroids](/wiki/Ergogenic_use_of_anabolic_steroids "Ergogenic use of anabolic steroids"), is believed to be relatively commonplace among competitors in jiu-jitsu tournaments and has sparked letters and public statements by some of jiu-jitsu's top competitors, including [Rodrigo Medeiros](/wiki/Rodrigo_Medeiros "Rodrigo Medeiros") and [Caio Terra](/wiki/Caio_Terra "Caio Terra").[[79]](#cite_note-79) In response, the [IBJJF](/wiki/IBJJF "IBJJF") began testing for performance-enhancing drugs at IBJJF sponsored events, starting with the 2013 Pan American Championship.[[80]](#cite_note-80) However, most other tournaments tend to lack PED testing.

### Cervical and spinal lock "neck cranks"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Cervical and spinal lock \"neck cranks\"")]

A [spinal lock](/wiki/Spinal_lock "Spinal lock") is a multiple [joint lock](/wiki/Joint_lock "Joint lock") applied to the [spinal column](/wiki/Spinal_column "Spinal column"), which is performed by forcing the spine beyond its normal ranges of motion. This is typically done by bending or twisting the head or upper body into abnormal positions. Commonly, spinal locks might [strain](/wiki/Strain_(injury) "Strain (injury)") the [spinal](/wiki/Vertebral_column "Vertebral column") [musculature](/wiki/Muscle "Muscle") or result in a mild spinal [sprain](/wiki/Sprain "Sprain"), while a forcefully and/or suddenly applied spinal lock may cause severe [ligament](/wiki/Ligament "Ligament") damage or damage to the [vertebrae](/wiki/Vertebrae "Vertebrae"), and possibly result in serious [spinal cord injury](/wiki/Spinal_cord_injury "Spinal cord injury"), [strokes](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"), or death. Spinal locks and cervical locks are forbidden in gi jiu-jitsu,[[30]](#cite_note-ibjjf.org-30) amateur MMA,[[32]](#cite_note-abcboxing.com-32) multiple forms of no-gi jiu-jitsu,[[30]](#cite_note-ibjjf.org-30) Judo,[[33]](#cite_note-intjudo.eu-33) and other martial arts[[34]](#cite_note-usgrappling.com-34) due to its illegal nature and express purpose to cause serious, irrevocable bodily injury, paralysis, and death.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) The forceful application of chokes such as the [Rear Naked Choke](/wiki/Rear_Naked_Choke "Rear Naked Choke") and subsequent cranking of the neck whilst being choked can potentially lead to arterial dissection, which could lead to stroke.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Due care should be exercised when applying these chokes in sparring situations.

### Elbow injuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Elbow injuries")]

Elbow injuries commonly occur from arm bars, Kimuras, and takedowns with a fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH). These injuries include LCL sprain [of elbow], MCL sprain [of elbow], anterior sprain of distal biceps tendon, elbow dislocation, and posterior tenderness at olecranon.[[74]](#cite_note-:0-74)

### Knee injuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Knee injuries")]

Knee injuries occur from passing guard, takedowns, sweeps, direct pressure, and various stress with flexed knee. These injuries include LCL sprain [of knee], MCL sprain [of knee], and lateral meniscus tear.[[74]](#cite_note-:0-74)

### Foot and ankle injuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Foot and ankle injuries")]

Foot and ankle injuries occur from pushing forward, takedowns, and footlocks. These injuries include ankle ATFL sprain, turf toe, and hyper flexion sprain of great toe.[[74]](#cite_note-:0-74)

### Finger injuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Finger injuries")]

Finger injuries occur from getting caught in the gi, applying arm bars, and blocking opponents. These injuries include sprains, dislocations, and fractures.[[74]](#cite_note-:0-74)

### Shoulder injuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Shoulder injuries")]

Shoulder injuries occur from Kimuras, takedowns with a fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH), and pushing opponents. These injuries include acromioclavicular separation, anterior dislocation, and anterior subluxation (partial dislocation).[[74]](#cite_note-:0-74)

### Neck injuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Neck injuries")]

Neck injuries can occur from "neck cranks", arm triangle chokes and triangle chokes, amongst other neck focussed submissions (especially when resisting them). These injuries include cervical strains.[[74]](#cite_note-:0-74)

### Rehab

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brazilian_jiu-jitsu&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Rehab")]

Depending on the severity, many injuries require evaluation by a professional, usually a primary care provider, sports medicine physician, orthopedic surgeon, or physical therapist. Other injuries may be treated with a home exercise program.
